structure.description: Search input accepts a small DSL with operators (tag:#x, file:y, path:z, line:N) alongside free text. Operators auto-complete from the active vocabulary (tag list, folder list). Results panel renders matched terms highlighted; the parsed AST is reflected as filter chips so user can see what the system understood. Power-tier of inline-search: same trigger surface, richer expressivity.

rationale.hypothesis: Filter-only UIs (chips, dropdowns) hit a ceiling at ~5 conjunctive predicates — beyond that, users need to type. A typed DSL with autocomplete is more expressive than chips and more tractable than raw regex. The reflection step (parsed AST → chips) closes the loop: user sees what the system parsed, can edit by clicking chips back into edit. Builds on inline-search rather than replacing it.

evidence.description: Search DSL: tag:, file:, path:, line:, block:, section:, task:, ignore-case: — over a dozen operators
Issue search DSL is the canonical reference for compositional filter languages
Filter bar mixes typed DSL with chip suggestions

counterexample.description: Free-text-only; DSL operators (site:, after:) exist but are hidden from the main UX — different audience

shouldMatch.reason: Notion search has filters that could be expressed as DSL

shouldNotMatch.reason: Search is small-vocab + lifecycle-driven; DSL is over-engineering
